questions.question: List four toxic heavy metals found in e-waste and state one health hazard for each.
keyPoints: Lead - neurological damage, kidney problems
Mercury - nervous system damage, Minamata disease
Cadmium - kidney damage, Itai-itai disease (bone degeneration)
Chromium (Cr VI) - lung cancer, DNA mutation

questions.question: Explain the concept of bioaccumulation with respect to e-waste toxins.
keyPoints: Gradual buildup of toxic substances in living organisms
Rate of intake exceeds rate of elimination
Concentration increases up the food chain
Example: dioxins accumulating in fish consumed by humans

questions.question: What are the main concerns with informal e-waste recycling practices?
keyPoints: Exposure to toxic substances without protection
Burning releases dioxins and furans
Acid leaching contaminates water sources
Health risks include respiratory diseases, neurological damage
No safety equipment for workers

questions.question: Differentiate between primary and secondary recycling of e-waste.
keyPoints: Primary: manual/mechanical disassembly, no chemical treatment
Primary: recovers raw materials like metals, plastics, glass
Secondary: uses smelting/refining processes
Secondary: involves pyrometallurgical or hydrometallurgical methods

questions.question: State the objectives of the Basel Convention regarding e-waste.
keyPoints: Regulate transboundary movement of hazardous wastes
Prevent dumping of hazardous waste on developing countries
Ensure environmentally sound disposal of hazardous wastes
Promote cooperation between parties for waste management

questions.question: What is Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and how does it help in e-waste management?
keyPoints: Policy where manufacturers bear responsibility for end-of-life products
Encourages design for recyclability
Financial responsibility for collection and disposal
Promotes take-back programs and proper recycling infrastructure

questions.question: Describe the environmental and health impacts of improper e-waste disposal. Suggest measures for proper e-waste management in India.
keyPoints: Environmental: Soil contamination from heavy metals, water pollution, air pollution from burning
Health: Neurological damage, kidney problems, respiratory diseases, cancer
Improper disposal leads to bioaccumulation in food chain
Measures: Implement E-Waste Rules 2016 effectively, promote formal recycling sectors, EPR implementation, public awareness, proper collection centers, strict regulation of informal sector

questions.question: Explain the hazardous properties of PCBs and Brominated Flame Retardants (BFRs) found in e-waste. Why are they considered persistent organic pollutants?
keyPoints: PCBs: toxic, persist in environment, bioaccumulate, cause cancer and immune system disorders
BFRs: endocrine disruption, immune system effects, persist in environment
Both resist degradation - persist for decades
Both bioaccumulate in fatty tissues
Both can travel long distances through air and water
Both toxic at very low concentrations

questions.question: With reference to e-waste management, explain: (i) WEEE Directive (ii) Steps in mechanical recycling process (iii) Role of EPR in sustainable e-waste management.
keyPoints: (i) WEEE: EU directive setting collection/recycling targets, producer responsibility, proper e-waste handling
(ii) Mechanical: Collection → Dismantling → Shredding → Magnetic separation → Eddy current separation → Density separation → Material recovery
(iii) EPR: Manufacturers responsible for end-of-life, design for environment, take-back schemes, financial provisions for recycling

questions.question: Discuss the health hazards associated with mercury, lead, and cadmium exposure from e-waste. How does informal recycling exacerbate these hazards?
keyPoints: Mercury: neurological damage, Minamata disease, birth defects
Lead: cognitive impairment in children, kidney damage, hypertension
Cadmium: Itai-itai disease, kidney damage, bone demineralization
Informal recycling: Open burning releases mercury vapor
Acid baths leach metals into water
No protective equipment for workers
Children and pregnant women especially vulnerable
No containment of toxic releases to environment
